coredata.dc:title: Effect of the oil content on green hydrogen production from produced water using carbon quantum dots as a disruptive nanolectrolyte
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC.
coredata.dc:creator: Ríos, Ever Herrera
Guzmán, Juan D.
Ribadeneira, Rafael
Bailón-García, Esther
Acevedo, Elizabeth Rodríguez
Vélez, Fredy
Franco, Camilo A.
Riazi, Masoud
Cortes, Farid B.
coredata.dc:description: Considering that high-purity fresh water is employed in the green production of hydrogen via commercial water electrolyzers and that over 80% of the global population faces significant water safety risks, it is essential to explore alternative water sources. One such alternative is the use of produced water from oil extraction processes. Hence, the main objective of this study is to evaluate the effect of the oil content of oilfield production water on the production of green hydrogen by electrolysis in the presence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). For this purpose, various electrochemical techniques, such as linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and potentiometry, were used to identify the effect of crude oil during hydrogen production. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), mass spectrometry (MS), and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) were employed to discern the adsorption of crude oil onto the electrodes, quantify the gas fractions generated during the process and identify the functional groups present after the procedure, respectively. Also, optical microscopy was employed to follow the droplet size in the emulsion. The results show that using CQDs affects the Faradaic efficiency, which increases from 77% to 83% with the incorporation of CQDs. In the presence of CQDs, the effects generated by the presence of the oil are inhibited at low oil concentrations. On the contrary, hydrogen production increases by 10.0% (0.1 mL/min) with a faradaic efficiency of 83% and a half-cell efficiency of 41%, compared to the record obtained with the maximum concentration of emulsified crude oil (400 mg/L). The mean size of the initial emulsion droplets was 3.4 μm. At the end of the test, there was evidence of complete breakage of the emulsion due to the effect of the applied electric field. No evidence of adsorption of crude oil on graphite electrodes during electrolysis is observed based on the tests. It has been shown that green hydrogen production from crude oil production water is feasible due to the proposed disruptive electrolytes in the produced water, which inhibit the effect of oil content in the O/W emulsion. This allows the implementation of a new green energy production initiative aligned with the global goal of achieving net zero emissions (NZE) by 2050. The current investigation presents a prospective alternative for harnessing the 18 kW electrical energy potential employed within emulsion-breaking processes within a Colombian field for treatment of around 1000 bpd. This alternative offers a theoretical potential for hydrogen production, approximating 7.36 kW, thus representing a promising opportunity for practical field deployment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Crude oil
CQDs
Electrolysis
Graphite
Hydrogen
Produced water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of crude oil and CQDs in cyclic voltammetry and potentiometry
Effect of crude oil and CQDs on chlorine evolution reaction (CER)
Effect of crude oil content on CER
Effect of the presence of CQDs on CER
Effect of crude oil content on HER
Effect of the presence of CQDs on HER
Produced gas analysis in the absence and presence of CQDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministerio de Ciencia, Tecnología e Innovación

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by Fondo Francisco Jos\u00E9 de Caldas, MINCIENCIAS and Agencia Nacional de hidrocarburos (ANH) through contract No. 112721-282-2023 (Project 282-2023) with Universidad Nacional de Colombia \u2013 Sede Medell\u00EDn and PAREX RESOURCES COLOMBIA AG SUCURSAL.

Hence, new alternatives and novel technologies should be developed to improve efficiency and reduce the associated problems during hydrogen production. The material used to produce hydrogen should have a large active surface area to aid in the kinetics of electron transport. This is easily accessible in carbon-based zero-dimensional (0D) and two-dimensional (2D) materials. Particularly for 0D materials, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the surface electric potential is strong, the surface area is vast, the dispersion is excellently, lowering the intermediate dissociation energy, and surface functional groups such as \u2013COOH, -OH, \u2013C[dbnd]O resulting in the enhancement of kinetics of electrocatalysis [39,40]. Supported nanoparticles on working electrodes, such as those based on graphene oxide and CQDs have been used, as they increase the electrical conductivity and have a great capacity to absorb energy due to their good characteristics such as large surface area, multiple active edge and defect sites, rapid electron transfer and electron reservoir properties [41\u201344]. The only reported work in which electrolyte nanofluids are used was performed by Wei et al. [45] using carbon black nanoparticles at a concentration of 0.1 % dispersed in sodium dodecyl sulfate in the presence of light, increasing the hydrogen production from pure water by about 30.4% [45,46].This study was funded by Fondo Francisco Jos\u00E9 de Caldas, MINCIENCIAS and Agencia Nacional de hidrocarburos (ANH) through contract No. 112721-282-2023 (Project 282\u20132023) with Universidad Nacional de Colombia \u2013 Sede Medell\u00EDn and PAREX RESOURCES COLOMBIA AG SUCURSAL.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cyclic voltammetry for four concentrations of crude oil with a scan speed set at 20 mV/s a) without CQDs and b) with CQDs. Potentiometry for 23 h to evaluate the system behavior, including the production flow rate c) without and d) with CQDs.
a) Linear sweep voltammetry curves in the potential range of 1.0 V and 2 V and b) Tafel plot for chlorine evolution reaction (CER) for the emulsions with different oil concentrations.

a) Linear sweep voltammetry curves in the potential range of 1.0 V and 2 V and b) Tafel plot for chlorine evolution reaction (CER) for the emulsions with different oil concentrations in the presence of 100 mg/L of CQDs.
a) Linear sweep voltammetry curves for the potential range between −1.0 and −2.0 V and b) Tafel plot for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) for the emulsions with different oil concentrations.

a) Linear sweep voltammetry curves for the potential range between −1.0 and −2.0 V and b) Tafel plot for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) for the emulsions with different oil concentrations in the presence of 100 mg/L of CQDs.

Mass percentage of gas produced from different emulsions with oil concentrations of 0, 10, 100, and 400 mg/L in water a) without and b) with CQDs. c) Cathodic resistance and d) anodic resistance for the emulsions in the absence and presence of CQDs.
FTIR spectra of the emulsions with different oil concentrations of 0, 10, 100, and 400 mg/L with and without CQDs at the end of each test.
Micrographs of a) stock emulsion at 400 mg/L before electrolysis and b) solutions with initial oil concentration of b) 10 mg/L, c) 100 mg/L and d) 400 mg/L after electrolysis with CQDs.
Thermogravimetric analysis for the crude oil and graphite electrodes before and after the potentiometry test.
Schematic of separation process installed in an oilfield.
Starting Conditions for the oil in water emulsions in the presence and absence of CQDs.
Faradaic efficiency of hydrogen (gas) and chlorine (gas) in the presence and absence of crude oil and CQDs.

head.ce:title: Effect of the oil content on green hydrogen production from produced water using carbon quantum dots as a disruptive nanolectrolyte

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Considering that high-purity fresh water is employed in the green production of hydrogen via commercial water electrolyzers and that over 80% of the global population faces significant water safety risks, it is essential to explore alternative water sources. One such alternative is the use of produced water from oil extraction processes. Hence, the main objective of this study is to evaluate the effect of the oil content of oilfield production water on the production of green hydrogen by electrolysis in the presence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). For this purpose, various electrochemical techniques, such as linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and potentiometry, were used to identify the effect of crude oil during hydrogen production. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), mass spectrometry (MS), and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) were employed to discern the adsorption of crude oil onto the electrodes, quantify the gas fractions generated during the process and identify the functional groups present after the procedure, respectively. Also, optical microscopy was employed to follow the droplet size in the emulsion. The results show that using CQDs affects the Faradaic efficiency, which increases from 77% to 83% with the incorporation of CQDs. In the presence of CQDs, the effects generated by the presence of the oil are inhibited at low oil concentrations.

On the contrary, hydrogen production increases by 10.0% (0.1 mL/min) with a faradaic efficiency of 83% and a half-cell efficiency of 41%, compared to the record obtained with the maximum concentration of emulsified crude oil (400 mg/L). The mean size of the initial emulsion droplets was 3.4 μm. At the end of the test, there was evidence of complete breakage of the emulsion due to the effect of the applied electric field. No evidence of adsorption of crude oil on graphite electrodes during electrolysis is observed based on the tests. It has been shown that green hydrogen production from crude oil production water is feasible due to the proposed disruptive electrolytes in the produced water, which inhibit the effect of oil content in the O/W emulsion. This allows the implementation of a new green energy production initiative aligned with the global goal of achieving net zero emissions (NZE) by 2050. The current investigation presents a prospective alternative for harnessing the 18 kW electrical energy potential employed within emulsion-breaking processes within a Colombian field for treatment of around 1000 bpd. This alternative offers a theoretical potential for hydrogen production, approximating 7.36 kW, thus representing a promising opportunity for practical field deployment.

ce:section.ce:para: With the world's population rising, so does the energy demand, which has had a major impact on climate change. As more energy is consumed, greenhouse gases (GHG) are emitted, leading to global warming [1]. According to the International Energy Agency (IEA), global energy consumption has increased over the last decade at an average annual growth rate of 1.4% [2]. For this reason, new and cleaner technologies have been proposed to overcome the energy demand with low GHG emissions. As a carbon-free energy carrier, hydrogen enables a materially closed and emission-free cycle using the conversion with electrochemical cells [3–5]. The hydrogen economy, which encompasses its production, storage, and conversion to electricity using renewable sources, has been widely studied [5,6] and is considered a promising solution for the future of energy [7]. Hence, hydrogen has become of particular interest in the search for energy alternatives due to its high calorific value. Hydrogen is currently used in various applications, such as refining, steel, ammonia, and methanol production [8,9]. Green hydrogen is an energy carrier with a broad range of industrial and energetic uses and a long-term storage capacity [10]. Its advantages and disadvantages come from its energy properties. Several types of hydrogen production can be categorized into different colors depending on the kind of energy used. These categories include gray, blue, and green hydrogen, among others [11].

Green hydrogen production involves the use of renewable energies and is achieved through the process of electrolysis. Consequently, the potential of hydrogen extends beyond its role as an alternative energy source; it also encompasses the significant benefit of zero CO2 emissions. Furthermore, green hydrogen offers a viable option for energy storage, particularly utilizing extra energy generated from renewable sources during peak production periods [12] based on high-purity fresh water in commercial water electrolyzers. In contrast, gray and blue hydrogen use fossil fuels for H2 production, with the difference that blue hydrogen uses CO2 capture mechanisms. Another variety called aqua hydrogen is produced from fossil fuels but inhibits CO2 emissions [13–15].

Hence, different strategies have been developed to improve green hydrogen production efficiency, using different electrode materials and electrolytes to optimize their designs [16–23]. One of the challenges in this process is the type of water used since it requires high purity to avoid blockage and deterioration of the electrodes. Nevertheless, fresh water is scarce in many areas, with over 80% of the global population facing significant water security challenges [1].

Currently, some studies focus on using seawater for hydrogen production. Nevertheless, the possibility of sustaining the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) versus the chlorine evolution reaction (CER) at the anode is challenging due to corrosive processes [24,25]. Several methods for hydrogen production via electrolysis are prominently discussed, including alkaline water electrolysis (AWE), proton exchange membrane electrolysis (PEM), and solid oxide electrolysis (SOE), all noted for their high efficiency [24,26–32]. It is worth mentioning the study reported by Rustana et al. [33], which describes how hydrogen production from seawater is feasible at voltages below 1.5 V, considering the thermodynamic voltage required for electrolysis is 1.23 V [33–35]. For this reason, looking for alternative uses of water that may be a waste for some industries provides opportunities to expand the knowledge and application of disruptive technologies for H2 production.

An alternative water source is found in the oil and gas industry, where oil wells can produce huge amounts of water. For instance, about 1.7 MMbbl/day of water can be produced in a field in Colombia [36]. However, the water produced from oilfields may contain different contaminants that affect hydrogen production, such as Mg and Ca salts that adhere to the electrode surface and emulsified crude oil. Several studies have investigated the use of industrial wastewater or vegetable oil as feedstock for electrolysis and have found that the presence of vegetable oils can extend the life of electrodes by creating a protective layer on their surface, which prevents corrosive processes [37,38].

Hence, new alternatives and novel technologies should be developed to improve efficiency and reduce the associated problems during hydrogen production. The material used to produce hydrogen should have a large active surface area to aid in the kinetics of electron transport. This is easily accessible in carbon-based zero-dimensional (0D) and two-dimensional (2D) materials. Particularly for 0D materials, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the surface electric potential is strong, the surface area is vast, the dispersion is excellently, lowering the intermediate dissociation energy, and surface functional groups such as –COOH, -OH, –CO resulting in the enhancement of kinetics of electrocatalysis [39,40]. Supported nanoparticles on working electrodes, such as those based on graphene oxide and CQDs have been used, as they increase the electrical conductivity and have a great capacity to absorb energy due to their good characteristics such as large surface area, multiple active edge and defect sites, rapid electron transfer and electron reservoir properties [41–44]. The only reported work in which electrolyte nanofluids are used was performed by Wei et al. [45] using carbon black nanoparticles at a concentration of 0.1 % dispersed in sodium dodecyl sulfate in the presence of light, increasing the hydrogen production from pure water by about 30.4% [45,46].

However, to the best of our knowledge, no studies are reported in the specialized literature evaluating green hydrogen production by electrolysis using water produced from oil wells or electrolytic nanofluids such as CQDs. Therefore, the main objective of this study is to evaluate the effect of the oil content in the produced water for green hydrogen production and provide a baseline scenario with this technology. Additionally, using CQDs in produced water like a disruptive nanoelectrolyte will be analyzed, considering its conductivity and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), which favor properties to improve the studied system.

During the study, the results of hydrogen production and chlorine production with and without CQDs were evaluated to identify the effect of the crude oil on the electrolyte and possible associated reaction mechanisms. This study opens a wider landscape of the production of green hydrogen and the role of the oil and gas industry in the energy transition.

Deionized water, NaCl (99.5%, LOBA Chemie, India), a Colombian intermediate crude oil with API gravity of 25.7° and Tween 80 (Protokimica, Colombia) were used for the preparation of crude oil in water (O/W) emulsion. The working crude oil has a content of saturates, aromatics, resins and asphaltenes of 48.9%, 24.5%, 24.5%, and 2.1%, respectively. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized according to a previous study [43]. The synthesis of CQDs using the employed method can approximate a cost of 170 USD per kilogram. However, employing alternative production methods using waste materials can reduce the cost to 50 USD per kilogram, as described by Franco et al. [43]. The main properties of the employed CQDs are summarized in Table 1 .

This study explores the feasibility of producing green hydrogen from oil production waters by utilizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to mitigate the adverse impact on system conductivity. CQDs are chosen for their conductive and advantageous properties in the HER, well-documented in existing literature [47]. This work is an initial step towards developing a viable approach for green hydrogen production from oil production waters. To accomplish this, a variety of analytical techniques such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), potentiometry (P), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), digital microscopy (DM), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and mass spectrometry (MS) were applied in triplicate to gain insight into the reaction mechanisms associated with the system, both in the presence and absence of crude oil.

A 0.25 M NaCl brine was prepared with deionized water with continuous stirring at 600 rpm for emulsion preparation. The crude oil-in-water (O/W) emulsions were prepared by mixing the brine and crude oil at 16000 rpm for 30 min to form a stock emulsion of 400 mg/L. Emulsion stability was ensured by monitoring through microscopy (Motic BA310) every 24 h, removing the supernatant, and finishing with filtration every 48 h [36]. Then, the emulsion was diluted to 10 and 100 mg/L of oil.

The experimental setup was designed in a sterile glass container, using an Ag/AgCl reference electrode (Gamry Instrumentals, Warminster, USA) and an INTERFACE 1000 potentiostat (Gamry Instrumentals, Warminster, USA). Considering the content of NA+ and Cl− ions in the electrolyte, an inert electrode is required. For this reason, given its good conductivity and low cost, parallel graphite electrodes were used. Each electrode exposes an effective geometric area of 22.5 cm2, with a separation of 3.5 cm between electrodes. The assembly and reference electrode were validated with the results reported by Quan et al. [48]. The cyclic voltammetry curves for graphite electrodes in NaCl 1 M solution were obtained at 2, 5 10, 20, and 50 mV/s. In addition, the sweep speed in the study was set at 20 mV/s with a working potential of −2 V. The CQDs were synthesized using a microwave-assisted carbonization methodology, adapted from that reported by Franco et al. [43].

The flow is continuously measured using a GFM17 mass flow meter (AALBORG INSTRUMENTS & CONTROLS, INC. Canada). The gases were collected and characterized using a OmniStar mass spectrometer (Pfeiffer Vacuum, Asslar, Germany). Additionally, an IRaffinity-1 FTIR spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was employed for FTIR analysis on the working electrolyte before and after each test to identify if there was any change in the composition of the electrolyte as a consequence of the reaction mechanisms present in the system during the electrolysis. Flow rate, pH, conductivity, and temperature were recorded before the process to evaluate the system's repeatability.

The chronoamperometry test times were set for 21 h for crude oil concentrations of 0, 10, 100, and 400 mg/L. Three test voltages of 2, 3, and 4V were previously evaluated, and the one that provided a sufficient gas bubble production and showed the system's least heating was selected. For this case, a fixed potential of -2V was used, since it allowed abundant production of bubbles above the reaction initiation potential, where the temperature increase inside the cell at the end of the test was approximately 1.0 °C. The above is because heat generation within the cell contributes to an increased resistance to charge transfer in the electrolyte, primarily due to the resistance encountered by electrons flowing through the system, manifesting as ohmic overpotential [49]. It was decided to use lower current densities suitable for the experimental cell dimensions as a methodological adaptation. This approach also ensured that the gas production was compatible with the operational capabilities of the equipment available for this study. It is noteworthy that enhanced performance at elevated temperatures has been documented, and typically, production of water surface at temperatures exceeding ambient conditions. Thus, the formation of water vapor that could cause deviations in the quantification of the gases produced was reduced.

Efficiency calculations were carried out considering Faraday's first law, a consequence of the law of conservation of matter, which states that the released mass is directly proportional to the electric charge transferred during electrolysis [50]. Therefore, if during a time t (s) a current flow I (A) circulates in the system, the amount of moles of generated hydrogen is [50]:
(1)
n =
I t
2 F
with F = 96485 (C/mol). This amount of hydrogen would be produced with a faradaic efficiency of 100%. Determining faradaic efficiency involves measuring the mass or volume of produced hydrogen and comparing it to the theoretical value derived from the ideal gas equation, considering that hydrogen behaves as an ideal gas under ambient conditions. The calculated theoretical volume can be obtained by applying Equation (2) for the evaluated pressure P and temperature T conditions:
(2)
V
t h e o r e t i c a l
=
n R T
P
where with R = 0.082057 (atm L/mol K). The faradaic efficiency was calculated by comparing the real and maximum theoretical values in Equation (3):
(3)
F a r a d a i c
e f f i c i e n c y =
V
R e a l
V
t h e o r e t i c a l
× 100

The
V
R e a l
was determined with a flow meter as mentioned in Section 2.2.2 and the result was validated provoking combustion.

The emulsion in the absence and presence of CQDs was characterized through different techniques. The sizes of droplets in the stock emulsion ranged from 1.2 μm to 4.5 μm, with a mean diameter of 3.4 μm. It is worth mentioning that there was no change in the initial droplet size when adding the CQDs. Also, the conductivity, pH, and temperature at the beginning of the tests are summarized in Table 2 . It can be observed in Table 2 that the experimental conditions and emulsion properties are maintained when CQDs are added to the emulsion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of crude oil and CQDs in cyclic voltammetry and potentiometry
ce:section.ce:para: The system's efficiency during the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and chlorine evolution reaction (CER) was analyzed using chronoamperometry and Faradaic efficiency. Tafel curves were obtained to identify the overpotential requirement to reach a reference current density in all four evaluated systems and to recognize the effect of crude oil during the process. Fig. 1 a shows the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves for the studied crude oil concentrations with and without CQDs. The scan rate was fixed at 20 mV/s, the initial potential (Eo) was −2 V, the cathodic cut-off potential (Ec) was −2 V, and the anodic cut-off potential (Ea) was 2 V. The systems, both with and without CQD, present an oxidation peak at 0.71 V. This peak could correspond to the theoretical potential for H2O2 production (Eo = 0.695 V) as indicated by Sethuraman et al. [51] and considering the composition of our systems.

Since the durability of the electrodes is always an important step in developing new processes such as the one implemented in this study, Fig. 1b shows the response in the potentiometry during 21 h for the graphite electrodes and the four concentrations of crude oil with and without CQDs. An increase in the current values over time can be appreciated. This is because the worked solution is not fed back, and considering that the electrolyte is becoming richer in OH- anions as the electrolysis progresses, the conductivity response favors the current passage. This allows inferring that there is no considerable impact on gas production for the worked concentrations of crude oil. In the presence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), there is evidence of an improvement in the reduction and oxidation reactions since a lower overpotential (mV) is required to achieve a reference current density [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of crude oil and CQDs on chlorine evolution reaction (CER)
Effect of crude oil content on CER
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a shows the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curve within the potential range of 1.0–2.0 V for the chlorine evolution reaction (CER). The highest potential recorded in the systems was obtained without crude oil (169.5 mA). With the addition of crude oil, a slight decrease in current was observed for concentrations of 10 mg/L (168.2 mA), 100 mg/L (167.2 mA), and 400 mg/L (161.8 mA) while maintaining the percentage of chlorine produced at 7% in all cases. These results suggest that the presence of crude oil in the systems without CQDs reduces conductivity as the concentration of crude oil increases, but it does not influence the fraction of chlorine produced. This behavior is consistent with the conductivity data in Table 2, where higher crude oil concentrations correspond to decreased conductivity or increased solution resistivity [53].

The CER aligns with the theoretical value of chlorine production. The amount of generated gas during the analysis supports the chosen working potential, as values above 1.8 V resulted in significant gas evolution on the electrodes. The Tafel diagram in Fig. 2b from the LSV curve demonstrates low kinetics for this electrode type. Furthermore, the logarithm of current density for CER at oil concentrations of 0, 10, 100, and 400 mg/L were 0.877, 0.874, 0.871, and 0.857 Log(mA/cm2), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the presence of CQDs on CER
ce:section.ce:para: With the addition of 100 mg/L of CQDs in each of the evaluated systems, an improvement in the chlorine evolution reaction (CER) can be observed, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The Tafel diagram in Fig. 3b, elaborated from the LSV curve and the registration of the working electrode's geometric area, indicates low kinetics for this electrode type. In addition, it is observed that the speed and the determining step for CER for the different oil concentrations are the same with a value of ∼1.31 V. Meanwhile, the logarithm of current density were 0.92, 0.92, 0.92, and 0.86 Log(mA/cm2) for oil concentrations of 0, 10, 100 and 400 mg/L, respectively. These results indicate that, in the presence of CQDs, there is more current density for the reaction, which implies more H2 production. Future work is expected to identify changes in the activity, selectivity, and stability of the reaction due to other reactive species that may be present [54–57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of crude oil content on HER
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a shows the LSV curve for the potential range between −1.0 and −2.0 V for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Like CER, similar current density values of 0.798, 0.795, 0.792, and 0.778 Log(mA/cm2) are observed for oil concentrations of 0, 10, 100, and 400 mg/L, respectively. The Tafel plot in Fig. 4b, derived from the LSV curve, also indicates low kinetics for this electrode type, with the velocity and rate-determining step for HER.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the presence of CQDs on HER
ce:section.ce:para: When the CQDs are added to each system, a favorable response is evidenced regarding the current density for the same work potential. This increase indicates a greater number of species reacting at the cathode. The improvement of the catalytic activity of HER is due to the improvement in the electron transfer rates in solution because the surface of the CQDs is composed of a hydrophobic carbon core, which primarily contains C–C bonds. In contrast, the surfaces contain abundant hydrophilic functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl. The number of H+ ions increases when the CQDs are ionized under acidic conditions, accelerating ion transmission in the solution [58–60].

With the records of the anodic and cathodic reactions of the system, it can be inferred that the concentration of crude oil does not have a considerable implication for the potential of the system (Fig. 5 a). In addition, in terms of the quantified production efficiency concerning the hydrogen produced, it was an average of 80%. The faradaic efficiency shows an improvement of 7.7%, going from 77% in the system with crude oil to 83% in the presence of CQDs with 400 mg/L of crude oil. These results show that the impact on HER kinetics with crude oil in the system is inhibited by CQDs, as shown in Fig. 5b. At oil concentrations of 0, 10, 100, and 400 mg/L, the obtained values of current density were 0.842, 0.841, 0.840, and 0.785 Log(mA/cm2), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Produced gas analysis in the absence and presence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The gases produced were analyzed by a mass spectrometer, and the production flow was quantified using a flow meter. No changes in the fractions produced (93% hydrogen and 7% chlorine) are evident with and without crude oil. A 9% reduction in the total flow is observed when using a 400 mg/L oil concentration. This result could be associated with the drop in the system's conductivity with increased crude oil concentration [61].

Incorporating CQDs nanoelectrolytes significantly enhances the electrochemical process in electrolysis systems, especially those affected by crude oil emulsions. Initial observations indicate that at lower concentrations of emulsified crude oil in water, these contaminants do not markedly affect the total volume of gas produced nor alter the proportions of different gas fractions. However, as depicted in Fig. 6 a, increasing the concentration of crude oil results in a discernible decrease in the overall volume of gases produced, suggesting an adverse impact on the system's efficiency. The introduction of CQDs nanoelectrolytes into the system represents a pivotal change. With CQDs, there is not only a modification in the anodic and cathodic potentials but also, as shown in Fig. 6b, an enhancement in the total gas production, even in the presence of higher concentrations of crude oil. This suggests that CQDs act as inhibitors of the negative effects of crude oil. Their presence mitigates the reduction in H2 productivity typically seen with increasing crude oil concentrations, thus underscoring the role of nanoelectrolytes in maintaining and even enhancing system performance under potentially deleterious conditions.

Moreover, the CQDs appear to be particularly effective at low crude oil concentrations, where their inhibitory effect is most pronounced, leading to greater overall gas production. This enhancement is attributed to the improved conductivity and possibly facilitated charge transfer processes afforded by the nanoscale properties of CQDs [52]. This will refine the understanding of how CQDs enhance electrolysis and help optimize the design and operational parameters of such systems to leverage the full potential of nanoelectrolytes.

In the HER and CER context, cathodic current and anodic current measurements were acquired at a fixed potential of −2.0 and 2.0 V, respectively, corresponding to a crude oil concentration of 400 mg/L. This specific potential was intentionally chosen due to its suboptimal performance and is intended to serve as a consistent benchmark across all experimental setups regarding resistance. The findings shown in Fig. 6c and d and quantified in terms of electrical resistance reveal a compelling trend. As the concentration of crude oil within the system increases, it becomes apparent that a higher overpotential is required to sustain an equivalent current density. This observed phenomenon can be attributed to the decreased electrolyte conductivity resulting from the presence of crude oil. Including CQDs (Fig. 6c and d) significantly enhances the system's conductivity. This improvement is directly attributed to the properties of the CQDs. Hence, the negative effects typically associated with the presence of crude oil in the system are substantially mitigated, especially at lower concentrations of emulsified crude oil. This observation underscores the effectiveness of CQDs in stabilizing the electrochemical environment and promoting more efficient reaction dynamics under potentially disruptive conditions.

Table 3 summarizes the faradaic efficiencies obtained. Notably, in the case of CER, a markedly low efficiency was observed as determined through gas collection analysis. The feed stream requires only NaCl to generate HOCl and OCl− ions [62]. The observed low faradaic efficiency for chlorine generation implies the occurrence of alternate electrochemical reactions. These reactions may be competing with the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). Additionally, hydrocarbons' degradation during the electrochemical reaction progression was meticulously monitored by quantifying the samples' chemical oxygen demand (COD) at various time intervals [63]. For a concentration of 400 mg/L of crude oil, the COD values recorded at 0, 3 and 6 h were 485 mg/L O2, 465 mg/L O2, and 445 mg/L O2, respectively. These results corroborate the system's low Faradaic efficiency.

Future research endeavors aim to elucidate and quantify these competing mechanisms, determining whether complete electrodegradation transpires or if the process forms simpler carbon chains. This follows the findings of Sharma et al. [63], which suggest that complete electrodegradation is achievable only under specific conditions, such as applying high potentials (12V) and using an oxidizing electrolyte like H2O2.

Although the results confirm that the proportions of gases produced remain constant, an increase in the total flow is observed. This increase suggests the presence of a larger reactant mass, which consequently leads to a higher concentration of Cl−, which is highly corrosive to the working electrodes [64]. FTIR analysis of the working electrolyte was performed before and after electrolysis, and each of the crude oil concentrations was employed to identify any change in the composition because of the system's reaction mechanisms; the results are shown in Fig. 7 . Four different bands can be observed in Fig. 7. The bands centered at ∼3300 cm−1 and ∼1635 cm−1 correspond to water in the system, and the one centered at ∼2100 cm−1 could correspond to the structure of the graphite electrodes [65,66]. However, the main difference is observed in the band centered at ∼700 cm−1, which may correspond to C–H out-of-plane vibrations [66]. This result could be related to the attachment of the crude oil to the surface of the electrode, as the transmittance decreases as the crude oil content in the sample increases. Nevertheless, this effect is inhibited when CQDs are included in the system, which could lead to higher electrode durability.

After electrolysis, the emulsion stability was also monitored for oil concentrations of 10, 100, and 400 mg/L in the presence of CQDs. Fig. 8 a shows the micrograph of the stock solution at 400 mg/L. Also, Fig. 8b, c, and 6d show the micrographs for the emulsions in the presence of CQDs. Fig. 8 shows that after the electrolysis process, there are no oil droplets in the system, which could be a synergistic effect between coalescence, separation, and oil degradation. The effect of electric current, more precisely direct current (DC), has a significant effect on the breaking of emulsions, having as a previous step the promotion of the coalescence of the droplets by reducing their interfacial tension. This effect depends on the emulsion components' nature, current intensity, and current application time [67].

The response to breaking the emulsions could be as efficient as commercial electrolizers. This is because the separation processes in commercial systems have numerous designs in which alternating current is used and can be useful with high water contents that favor charge transfer at an optimum frequency [68].

Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were conducted on the crude oil used in the experiment and the electrodes before and after the electrolysis process with and without CQDs. Fig. 9 shows the obtained TGA results. The results indicated a 96% mass loss for the crude oil at 340 °C. For the electrodes employed in 0 and 10 mg/L solutions, no significant change in mass was observed. However, for the electrodes used in 100 and 400 mg/L electrolytes, evidence of mass change was found, which could be attributed to the crude oil adherence or adsorption over the electrodes after the test was completed. In the presence of CQDs, it is observed that the crude oil content in the electrode is reduced, which can increase the efficiency of the system for H2 production. This observation aligns with the results of cyclic voltammetry and mass spectrometry tests, which did not reveal any byproducts during the process.

For an oilfield of reference with an approximate crude oil production of 2000 bbl/day, a free water knock-out (FWKO) separator is used in the primary separation process. The schematic of the system installed in the field is shown in Fig. 10 . Subsequently, the reservoir fluids undergo electric charges in an electrostatic separator with steel cells that help break down stronger emulsions and recover more crude oil in the field. This electrostatic separator has an electrical consumption of 18 kW. Comparing this consumption with the results obtained in this study, considering the semi-cell for the worst system, a theoretical efficiency of approximately 41% can be obtained even when the process was not initially designed to be efficient. In this sense, there is an opportunity to generate green hydrogen in a process that has been used only for crude oil separation in oil fields. This could potentially reduce operating costs by being able to recycle this energy through a fuel cell.

The findings indicate that utilizing produced water for hydrogen generation is viable and establishes a promising direction for further exploration into the enhancement possibilities afforded by nanoscale materials such as Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), which were implemented as electrolytic nanofluids. Meanwhile, the total gas production decreases with an increased concentration of crude oil, and the composition of the gas fractions remains unchanged. However, the Introduction of CQDs to the system yields beneficial effects, notably enhancing the total gas flow and favorably influencing the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) during the process.

Throughout the monitoring of the working solution, no emulsion droplets were detected in the electrolyte. Current circulation promotes the coalescence and rupture of emulsions, suggesting potential areas for future research on the impact of other electrolyte components, such as various commercial surfactants or the deployment of dispersed nanoparticles to diminish interfacial tension.

The improved conductivity observed within the system, attributed to the integration of CQDs, demonstrates that the adverse effects associated with the presence of crude oil can be mitigated, particularly at lower concentrations of emulsified crude oil. This observation underscores the significance of CQDs in enhancing the electrochemical performance and stability of the system under suboptimal conditions.

Although notable results have been achieved, there are still opportunities for improvement that need to be addressed, such as.
•
Development of a technical-economic and life cycle assessment to provide better insight into this technology.
•
Evaluation of reaction mechanisms involved in the oxidation reactions with CQDs in the electrolyte.
•
Development of materials with higher selectivity and catalytic properties for HER and inhibition properties for CER that allow for longer electrode life.
•
Optimization of the electrolytic cell design, recommendations in the function of the area, space between electrodes, and other crudes considering the compositional variation of hydrocarbons in the production time.

Ever Herrera Ríos: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Juan D. Guzmán: Investigation. Rafael Ribadeneira: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Esther Bailón-García: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Elizabeth Rodríguez Acevedo: Formal analysis. Fredy Vélez: Formal analysis. Camilo A. Franco: Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Masoud Riazi: Conceptualization, Data curation. Farid B. Cortes: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by Fondo Francisco José de Caldas, MINCIENCIAS and Agencia Nacional de hidrocarburos (ANH) through contract No. 112721-282-2023 (Project 282–2023) with Universidad Nacional de Colombia – Sede Medellín and PAREX RESOURCES COLOMBIA AG SUCURSAL.
